Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "/**\n * An in-memory collection of {@link RecordBatch} instances backed by a {@link ByteBuffer}.\n *\n * <p><strong>Usage:</strong> This class is used to:\n * <ul>\n *   <li>Read existing record batches from a buffer (deserialization from network/disk)</li>\n *   <li>Modify record batches in-place (e.g., timestamp updates during log compaction)</li>\n *   <li>Filter records according to retention policies</li>\n * </ul>\n * To create new record batches, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or one of the factory methods like\n * {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}.\n *\n * <p><strong>Thread Safety:</strong> MemoryRecords instances are <strong>not thread-safe</strong>. The underlying\n * buffer may be modified via {@link MutableRecordBatch} operations (e.g., timestamp rewrites). Concurrent access\n * must be externally synchronized, or use snapshot methods that return independent copies.\n *\n * <p><strong>Performance Characteristics:</strong>\n * <ul>\n *   <li>{@link #sizeInBytes()} is O(1), returning the buffer limit</li>\n *   <li>{@link #validBytes()} is O(n) in batch count, iterating to sum valid batch sizes</li>\n *   <li>{@link #batches()} returns a lazy iterable; batches are parsed on-demand</li>\n *   <li>{@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} processes all batches and may trigger\n *       decompression, allocation of output buffers, and recomputation of checksums</li>\n * </ul>\n *\n * <p><strong>Memory Layout:</strong> The buffer contains a sequence of concatenated record batches, each with\n * a 4-byte length prefix and CRC checksum. The {@link #validBytes()} method returns the size of complete,\n * uncorrupted batches; partial records at the end are ignored.\n *\n * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches\n * @see RecordBatch for individual batch access\n * @see MutableRecordBatch for in-place batch modifications\n */\npublic class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {\n    public static final MemoryRecords EMPTY = MemoryRecords.readableRecords(ByteBuffer.allocate(0));\n\n    private final ByteBuffer buffer;\n\n    private final Iterable<MutableRecordBatch> batches = this::batchIterator;\n\n    private int validBytes = -1;\n\n    // Construct a writable memory records\n    private MemoryRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {\n        Objects.requireNonNull(buffer, \"buffer should not be null\");\n        this.buffer = buffer;\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int sizeInBytes() {\n        return buffer.limit();\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int writeTo(TransferableChannel channel, int position, int length) throws IOException {\n        if (((long) position) + length > buffer.limit())\n            throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"position+length should not be greater than buffer.limit(), position: \"\n                    + position + \", length: \" + length + \", buffer.limit(): \" + buffer.limit());\n\n        return Utils.tryWriteTo(channel, position, length, buffer);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Write all records to the given channel, including any partial records at the end.\n     *\n     * <p><strong>Behavior:</strong> Writes the entire buffer contents to the channel in potentially multiple\n     * write operations, continuing until all bytes are written or an exception is raised. Uses the channel's\n     * {@link GatheringByteChannel#write(ByteBuffer)} method, which may write fewer bytes than requested.\n     *\n     * <p><strong>Buffer State:</strong> The underlying buffer's position is preserved using mark/reset, so this\n     * method has no side effects on the buffer state.\n     *\n     * <p><strong>Partial Records:</strong> Unlike {@link #validBytes()}, this method includes partial or potentially\n     * corrupt record batches at the end of the buffer.\n     *\n     * @param channel the {@link GatheringByteChannel} to write to (e.g., socket, file channel)\n     * @return the total number of bytes written\n     * @throws IOException if an I/O error occurs during writing\n     */\n    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {\n        buffer.mark();\n        int written = 0;\n        while (written < sizeInBytes())\n            written += channel.write(buffer);\n        buffer.reset();\n        return written;\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Get the total number of valid bytes in this message set, excluding any partial or corrupted trailing data.",
    "filePath": "/workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java",
    "numLines": 90,
    "startLine": 44,
    "totalLines": 955

    "content": "# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Documentation Summary\n\n**Repository:** apache/kafka\n**Date:** March 1, 2026\n**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document summarizes the comprehensive Javadoc documentation generated for Kafka's three critical record batch serialization classes:\n- `RecordBatch` (interface)\n- `DefaultRecordBatch` (magic v2+ implementation)\n- `MemoryRecords` (in-memory batch collection)\n\nThese classes form the foundation of Kafka's message encoding and are essential for wire protocol compatibility, log storage, and broker-side record processing (compaction, filtering).\n\n---\n\n## 1. RecordBatch (Interface)\n\n**File:** `RecordBatch.java` (lines 28-58)\n\n### Class-Level Documentation Enhanced\n\nAdded comprehensive class-level Javadoc that includes:\n\n- **Purpose:** Container for records using Kafka's binary encoding format\n- **Thread Safety:** Explicitly stated as immutable and thread-safe for read operations, with notes about MutableRecordBatch for broker use\n- **Role in Kafka Protocol:** Explains batch structure (header, records, CRC), batch-level compression, and producer metadata for exactly-once semantics\n- **Performance Characteristics:** Documents lazy decompression and streaming iteration for memory efficiency\n- **Cross-references:** Links to DefaultRecordBatch, MutableRecordBatch, and MemoryRecords\n\n### Methods Documented\n\n| Method | Enhancement |\n|--------|-------------|\n| `isValid()` | Added details on checksum verification cost and use cases |\n| `ensureValid()` | Documented exception behavior and relationship to isValid() |\n| `streamingIterator()` | Comprehensive performance guide for memory-efficient decompression; detailed BufferSupplier usage |\n| All existing methods | Preserved existing documentation; enhanced where needed |\n\n**Key Documentation Patterns:**\n- Buffer management and decompression efficiency\n- Producer metadata for duplicate detection\n- Offset/sequence preservation across log compaction\n- Timestamp handling (CREATE_TIME vs LOG_APPEND_TIME)\n\n---\n\n## 2. DefaultRecordBatch (Implementation)\n",

1. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
After
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records that encapsulates the binary encoding of Kafka messages.
 *
 * <p>In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted always of a single record
 * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2
 * and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.
 *
 * <p><strong>Thread Safety:</strong> Record batches are immutable once created and are thread-safe for
 * concurrent read operations. However, some implementations like {@link DefaultRecordBatch} may support
 * in-place mutations via {@link MutableRecordBatch} for specific use cases (e.g., updating timestamps during
 * log compaction).
 *
 * <p><strong>Role in Kafka Protocol:</strong> Record batches are fundamental to Kafka's wire format and storage.
 * Each batch contains:
 * <ul>
 *   <li>Header information: offsets, timestamps, compression type, producer ID/epoch for exactly-once semantics</li>
 *   <li>A collection of individual records with associated keys, values, and headers</li>
 *   <li>CRC checksum for integrity verification</li>
 * </ul>
 * The batch structure allows efficient compression at the batch level and preserves producer metadata needed
 * for deduplication and transaction management.
 *
 * <p><strong>Performance Characteristics:</strong> Iteration over records may trigger decompression if the batch
 * is compressed. Use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} for memory-efficient iteration over large
 * compressed batches, as it decompresses records on-demand rather than materializing the entire batch in memory.
 *
 * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the magic v2+ implementation
 * @see MutableRecordBatch for batches that support in-place updates
 * @se
... (truncated)
2. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Check whether the checksum of this batch is correct.
     *
     * @return true If so, false otherwise
     */
    boolean isValid();
After
    /**
     * Check whether the checksum of this batch is correct.
     *
     * <p>The checksum covers the batch header and all records. Use this method to verify batch integrity
     * after deserialization or transmission. Note that this method computes the checksum and compares it
     * to the stored value, so it has a performance cost proportional to batch size.
     *
     * @return {@code true} if the stored checksum matches the computed checksum, {@code false} otherwise
     */
    boolean isValid();
3. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Raise an exception if the checksum is not valid.
     */
    void ensureValid();
After
    /**
     * Verify the checksum of this batch, raising a {@link CorruptRecordException} if it is invalid.
     *
     * <p>This is a convenience method that combines the checks from {@link #isValid()} and {@link #sizeInBytes()},
     * raising an exception if either validation fails. Use this method when you want to fail fast on corrupt batches.
     *
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if the batch size is smaller than the minimum required overhead or if the
     *                                checksum does not match
     */
    void ensureValid();
4. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
After
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator that delays decompression of the record stream until records are requested via
     * {@link Iterator#next()}. This provides memory-efficient iteration over compressed batches at the cost of
     * decompression latency per record.
     *
     * <p><strong>Important:</strong> This iterator <em>must</em> be closed to release decompression resources.
     * If the record format does not support streaming decompression (e.g., uncompressed batches), an uncompressed
     * iterator may be returned; these should still be closed for API consistency.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> For small batches with large decompression buffers (e.g., 64 KB for LZ4),
     * the cost of allocating a buffer may dominate decompression time. A {@link BufferSupplier} that reuses buffers
     * across batches can significantly improve performance.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplies reusable ByteBuffers for decompression. Use
     *                                     {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} for one-time iteration or
     *                                     implementations that cache buffers for batch sequences.
     * @return a {@link CloseableIterator} that iterates over records in this batch; must be closed to release
     *         decompression resources
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
5. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence e
... (truncated)
After
/**
 * {@link RecordBatch} implementation for Kafka record format magic version 2 and above.
 *
 * <p><strong>Binary Format:</strong> This class implements the following record batch schema:
 * <pre>{@code
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64              # First offset in this batch
 *  Length => Int32                  # Size of batch data following the length field
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32    # Leader epoch at time of batch write
 *  Magic => Int8                    # Record format version (2+)
 *  CRC => Uint32                    # CRC-32C checksum (covers Attributes onward)
 *  Attributes => Int16              # Batch attributes (compression, transactional, etc.)
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32         # Offset of last record relative to BaseOffset
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64           # Timestamp of first record (or delete horizon)
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64            # Maximum timestamp in batch
 *  ProducerId => Int64              # Producer ID for idempotency/transactions
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16           # Producer epoch for idempotency/transactions
 *  BaseSequence => Int32            # Sequence number of first record
 *  RecordsCount => Int32            # Number of records in batch
 *  Records => [Record]              # Compressed or uncompressed record data
 * }</pre>
 *
 * <p><strong>Checksum Computation:</strong> The CRC field uses the CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial and covers
 * all data from the Attributes field through the end of the batch. The PartitionLeaderEpoch field is intentionally
 * excluded from the CRC to avoid recomputation whenever a broker assigns a new leader epoch.
 *
 * <p><strong>Compression:</strong> When the batch has a compression codec enabled (see Attributes), the record data
 * immediately following the RecordsCount 
... (truncated)
6. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas.
     *
     * @return The base timestamp
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
After
    /**
     * Get the base timestamp of the batch used to reconstruct individual record timestamps.
     *
     * <p>Record timestamps in a batch are stored as deltas relative to this base timestamp. The actual timestamp
     * of any record is computed as {@code baseTimestamp() + recordTimestampDelta}.
     *
     * <p>For CREATE_TIME batches, this is the timestamp of the first record. For LOG_APPEND_TIME batches, this
     * is typically the log append time. If the delete horizon flag is set, this field contains the delete horizon
     * timestamp (time at which tombstones should be removed).
     *
     * @return the base timestamp in milliseconds since epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} (-1) if the
     *         batch is empty
     * @see #maxTimestamp() for the maximum timestamp in this batch
     * @see #deleteHorizonMs() to check if this is a delete horizon
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
7. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {
        if (count() == 0)
            return Collections.emptyIterator();

        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,
        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint
        // can use `streamingIterator` at the cost of additional complexity
        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iterator = compressedIterator(BufferSupplier.NO_CACHING, false)) {
            List<Record> records = new ArrayList<>(count());
            while (iterator.hasNext())
                records.add(iterator.next());
            return records.iterator();
        }
    }
After
    /**
     * Return an iterator over all records in this batch.
     *
     * <p><strong>Behavior:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>For uncompressed batches, iterates directly over the buffer without allocation</li>
     *   <li>For compressed batches, fully decompresses and materializes all records in memory, then iterates</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>When to use:</strong> Use this method when all records need to be accessible at once and a
     * standard non-closeable iterator is required. For better memory efficiency with large compressed batches,
     * use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> For compressed batches, this method allocates a list to hold all records,
     * which may consume significant heap for large batches. Decompression overhead is incurred upfront rather than
     * on-demand.
     *
     * @return an {@link Iterator} over {@link Record} objects in this batch; empty iterator if batch is empty
     */
    @Override
    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {
        if (count() == 0)
            return Collections.emptyIterator();

        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,
        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint
        // can use `streamingIterator` at the cost of additional complexity
        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iterator = compressedIterator(BufferSupplier.NO_CACHING, false)) {
            List<Record> records = new ArrayList<>(count());
            while (iterator.hasNext())
                records.add(iterator.next());
            return records.iterator();
        }
    
... (truncated)
8. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (count() == 0) {
            return CloseableIterator.wrap(Collections.emptyIterator());
        }

        /*
         * For uncompressed iterator, it is actually not worth skipping key / value / headers at all since
         * its ByteBufferInputStream's skip() function is less efficient compared with just reading it actually
         * as it will allocate new byte array.
         */
        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // we define this to be a closable iterator so that caller (i.e. the log validator) needs to close it
        // while we can save memory footprint of not decompressing the full record set ahead of time
        return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, true);
    }
After
    /**
     * Return an iterator that skips over record key, value, and header data during decompression.
     *
     * <p><strong>Use Case:</strong> This is optimized for log validation and offset scanning where only record
     * metadata (offset, timestamp, compression type) is needed, not the actual payload.
     *
     * <p><strong>Behavior:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>For uncompressed batches, still reads all data (skip is not efficient for ByteBuffer)</li>
     *   <li>For compressed batches, decompresses on-demand and skips payload fields to reduce memory usage</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>Resource Management:</strong> The returned iterator should be closed to release decompression
     * resources, especially for compressed batches.
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier supplies reusable decompression buffers; use {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} if
     *                       buffers will not be reused across batches
     * @return a {@link CloseableIterator} over records with payloads skipped; must be closed to release resources
     */
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (count() == 0) {
            return CloseableIterator.wrap(Collections.emptyIterator());
        }

        /*
         * For uncompressed iterator, it is actually not worth skipping key / value / headers at all since
         * its ByteBufferInputStream's skip() function is less efficient compared with just reading it actually
         * as it will allocate new byte array.
         */
        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // we define this to be a closable iterator so that caller (i.e. the log validator) needs to close it
        // while we can sav

Before
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (isCompressed())
            return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false);
        else
            return uncompressedIterator();
    }
After
    /**
     * Return a memory-efficient streaming iterator that decompresses records on-demand.
     *
     * <p><strong>When to use:</strong> Prefer this method over {@link #iterator()} when processing large
     * compressed batches where memory efficiency is important. Decompression is performed lazily as records are
     * requested, rather than materializing the entire batch upfront.
     *
     * <p><strong>Behavior:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>For uncompressed batches, directly iterates over the buffer</li>
     *   <li>For compressed batches, wraps the buffer with a decompression stream and yields records on-demand</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>Resource Management:</strong> The iterator must be closed to release decompression stream
     * resources. Use try-with-resources:
     * <pre>{@code
     * try (CloseableIterator<Record> iter = batch.streamingIterator(bufferSupplier)) {
     *     while (iter.hasNext()) {
     *         Record record = iter.next();
     *         // process record
     *     }
     * }
     * }</pre>
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> The buffer supplier's reuse strategy significantly impacts performance.
     * A supplier that reuses buffers across many batches can improve throughput compared to allocating a new
     * decompression buffer per batch.
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier supplies reusable ByteBuffers for decompression; {@link BufferSupplier#create()} for
     *                       per-batch allocation, or a custom supplier that reuses buffers across multiple batches
     * @return a {@link CloseableIterator} over records; must be closed to release decompression resources
     */

    public CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {

10. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public void setLastOffset(long offset) {
        buffer.putLong(BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET, offset - lastOffsetDelta());
    }
After
    /**
     * Update the base offset of this batch to make its last offset equal to the specified value.
     *
     * <p>This method is used during log compaction to update the batch offset range without changing the
     * last offset delta (which must be preserved for duplicate checking). The new base offset is computed as
     * {@code lastOffset - lastOffsetDelta()}.
     *
     * <p><strong>Note:</strong> This method modifies the batch in-place and invalidates the CRC checksum.
     * Callers must recompute the CRC or ensure the batch is not transmitted without a valid checksum.
     *
     * @param offset the desired last offset for this batch (inclusive)
     */
    @Override
    public void setLastOffset(long offset) {
        buffer.putLong(BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET, offset - lastOffsetDelta());
    }
11. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public void setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType timestampType, long maxTimestamp) {
        long currentMaxTimestamp = maxTimestamp();
        // We don't need to recompute crc if the timestamp is not updated.
        if (timestampType() == timestampType && currentMaxTimestamp == maxTimestamp)
            return;

        byte attributes = computeAttributes(compressionType(), timestampType, isTransactional(), isControlBatch(), hasDeleteHorizonMs());
        buffer.putShort(ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET, attributes);
        buffer.putLong(MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET, maxTimestamp);
        long crc = computeChecksum();
        ByteUtils.writeUnsignedInt(buffer, CRC_OFFSET, crc);
    }
After
    /**
     * Update the maximum timestamp and timestamp type of this batch, recomputing the CRC if needed.
     *
     * <p><strong>Use Case:</strong> This is used during log compaction to update the maximum timestamp after
     * some records have been removed, or to change the timestamp type based on topic configuration.
     *
     * <p><strong>Optimization:</strong> If the timestamp type and max timestamp are unchanged, this method
     * returns without modifying the batch or recomputing the CRC.
     *
     * <p><strong>Side Effects:</strong> This method modifies the batch in-place:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Updates the Attributes field with the new timestamp type</li>
     *   <li>Updates the MaxTimestamp field</li>
     *   <li>Recomputes and updates the CRC checksum to cover the new values</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @param timestampType the new timestamp type (must not be {@link TimestampType#NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE})
     * @param maxTimestamp  the new maximum timestamp in milliseconds since epoch
     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the timestamp type is NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE
     */
    @Override
    public void setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType timestampType, long maxTimestamp) {
        long currentMaxTimestamp = maxTimestamp();
        // We don't need to recompute crc if the timestamp is not updated.
        if (timestampType() == timestampType && currentMaxTimestamp == maxTimestamp)
            return;

        byte attributes = computeAttributes(compressionType(), timestampType, isTransactional(), isControlBatch(), hasDeleteHorizonMs());
        buffer.putShort(ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET, attributes);
        buffer.putLong(MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET, maxTimestamp);
        long crc = computeChecksum();
        ByteUtils.writeUnsignedInt(buffer, CRC_OFFSET, crc);
    }

Before
    @Override
    public void setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int epoch) {
        buffer.putInt(PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET, epoch);
    }
After
    /**
     * Update the partition leader epoch of this batch.
     *
     * <p>The partition leader epoch is set by the broker receiving the batch and indicates which leader
     * wrote this batch to the log. This field is excluded from the CRC checksum, allowing it to be updated
     * without invalidating the batch.
     *
     * <p><strong>Note:</strong> This method modifies the batch in-place but does not recompute the CRC
     * (the CRC remains valid since this field is not covered by it).
     *
     * @param epoch the leader epoch when this batch was written to the log
     */
    @Override
    public void setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int epoch) {
        buffer.putInt(PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET, epoch);
    }

Before
/**
 * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used only for reading or
 * modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer see {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder},
 * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)} variants.
 */
public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {
After
/**
 * An in-memory collection of {@link RecordBatch} instances backed by a {@link ByteBuffer}.
 *
 * <p><strong>Usage:</strong> This class is used to:
 * <ul>
 *   <li>Read existing record batches from a buffer (deserialization from network/disk)</li>
 *   <li>Modify record batches in-place (e.g., timestamp updates during log compaction)</li>
 *   <li>Filter records according to retention policies</li>
 * </ul>
 * To create new record batches, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or one of the factory methods like
 * {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}.
 *
 * <p><strong>Thread Safety:</strong> MemoryRecords instances are <strong>not thread-safe</strong>. The underlying
 * buffer may be modified via {@link MutableRecordBatch} operations (e.g., timestamp rewrites). Concurrent access
 * must be externally synchronized, or use snapshot methods that return independent copies.
 *
 * <p><strong>Performance Characteristics:</strong>
 * <ul>
 *   <li>{@link #sizeInBytes()} is O(1), returning the buffer limit</li>
 *   <li>{@link #validBytes()} is O(n) in batch count, iterating to sum valid batch sizes</li>
 *   <li>{@link #batches()} returns a lazy iterable; batches are parsed on-demand</li>
 *   <li>{@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} processes all batches and may trigger
 *       decompression, allocation of output buffers, and recomputation of checksums</li>
 * </ul>
 *
 * <p><strong>Memory Layout:</strong> The buffer contains a sequence of concatenated record batches, each with
 * a 4-byte length prefix and CRC checksum. The {@link #validBytes()} method returns the size of complete,
 * uncorrupted batches; partial records at the end are ignored.
 *
 * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches
 * @see RecordBatch for individ
... (truncated)
14. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * The total number of bytes in this message set not including any partial, trailing messages. This
     * may be smaller than what is returned by {@link #sizeInBytes()}.
     * @return The number of valid bytes
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
After
    /**
     * Get the total number of valid bytes in this message set, excluding any partial or corrupted trailing data.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> This method iterates all batches to sum their sizes and caches the result.
     * Subsequent calls return the cached value in O(1) time. However, if the underlying buffer is modified
     * (via {@link MutableRecordBatch} updates), the cache is not invalidated and this method may return stale values.
     *
     * <p><strong>Difference from {@link #sizeInBytes()}:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>{@link #sizeInBytes()} returns the buffer limit (includes any partial/corrupt batches)</li>
     *   <li>{@code validBytes()} returns only complete, valid batch sizes</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @return the number of valid bytes in all complete batches; may be less than buffer limit if partial batches
     *         exist at the end
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
15. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
After
    /**
     * Filter records according to a retention policy, writing results to a destination buffer.
     *
     * <p><strong>Use Case:</strong> This is the primary method for log compaction, used to remove records that
     * don't match a retention policy (e.g., deleted keys, expired transactions). Supports:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Batch-level filtering (discard entire batches)</li>
     *   <li>Record-level filtering (keep only matching records)</li>
     *   <li>Batch reconstruction with updated timestamps and delete horizons</li>
     *   <li>Preservation of producer state (offsets, sequences) for duplicate detection</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance Tuning:</strong> The decompression buffer supplier significantly impacts performance,
     * especially for large compressed batches or many sequential batches:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>{@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} allocates new buffer per batch (simplest but slowest)</li>
     *   <li>Custom suppliers can reuse buffers across the entire filter operation</li>
     *   <li>For 64 KB LZ4 buffers, reuse can dominate decompression time on small batches</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>Output Buffer Management:</strong> If the filtered records don't fit in the destination buffer,
     * a new buffer is allocated and returned in {@link FilterResult#outputBuffer()}.
     *
     * <p><strong>Batch Metadata Preservation:</strong> For magic v2+ batches:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Original base/last offsets are preserved (required for duplicate detection)</li>
     *   <li>Original base/last sequence numbers are preserved</li>
     *   <li>Timestamps are rewritten as needed; MaxTimestamp recomputed for CREATE_TIME batches</li>
     *   <li>Delete horizons are set when filtering removes tombst
... (truncated)

     * Get the byte buffer that backs this instance for reading.

After
    /**
     * Get a duplicate of the underlying byte buffer for reading.
     *
     * <p><strong>Important:</strong> The returned buffer is a duplicate, not a view. Changes to the returned
     * buffer's position and limit do not affect this MemoryRecords instance. However, modifications to the
     * underlying byte array (if the buffer is not read-only) <em>will</em> be reflected in this instance.
     *
     * @return a duplicate of the backing ByteBuffer; can be read from independent of this instance
     */
    public ByteBuffer buffer() {
        return buffer.duplicate();
    }
17. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    @Override
    public MemoryRecords slice(int position, int size) {
        if (position < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid position: " + position + " in read from " + this);
        if (position > buffer.limit())
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Slice from position " + position + " exceeds end position of " + this);
        if (size < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid size: " + size + " in read from " + this);

        int availableBytes = Math.min(size, buffer.limit() - position);
        // As of now, clients module support Java11 hence can't use ByteBuffer::slice(position, size) method.
        // So we need to create a duplicate buffer and set the position and limit. Duplicate buffer
        // is backed by original bytes hence not the content but only the relative position and limit
        // are changed in the duplicate buffer. Once the position and limit are set, we can call the
        // slice method to get the sliced buffer, which is a backed by the original buffer with the
        // position reset to 0 and limit set to the size of the slice.
        ByteBuffer slicedBuffer = buffer.duplicate();
        slicedBuffer.position(position);
        slicedBuffer.limit(position + availableBytes);
        // Reset the position to 0 so that the sliced view has a relative position.
        slicedBuffer = slicedBuffer.slice();

        return readableRecords(slicedBuffer);
    }
After
    /**
     * Get a MemoryRecords view for a slice of the buffer.
     *
     * <p><strong>Slicing Behavior:</strong> The returned MemoryRecords wraps a sliced view of the underlying
     * buffer starting at the specified position with the specified maximum size. The slice is backed by the
     * original buffer's data; changes to the underlying byte array will be reflected in the slice.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> Creating a slice is O(1) and does not copy data. The sliced view is ready
     * for iterating or filtering immediately.
     *
     * <p><strong>Boundary Handling:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>If position + size exceeds the buffer limit, the slice is truncated to the buffer end</li>
     *   <li>Partial record batches at the end are included in the slice but ignored by batch iteration</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @param position the byte offset in the buffer where the slice begins (0-indexed)
     * @param size     the maximum number of bytes to include in the slice
     * @return a MemoryRecords view of the sliced buffer
     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if position is negative or exceeds the buffer limit, or if size is negative
     */
    @Override
    public MemoryRecords slice(int position, int size) {
        if (position < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid position: " + position + " in read from " + this);
        if (position > buffer.limit())
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Slice from position " + position + " exceeds end position of " + this);
        if (size < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid size: " + size + " in read from " + this);

        int availableBytes = Math.min(size, buffer.limit() - position);
        // As of now, clients module sup
... (truncated)
18. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Write all records to the given channel (including partial records).
     * @param channel The channel to write to
     * @return The number of bytes written
     * @throws IOException For any IO errors writing to the channel
     */
    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {
        buffer.mark();
        int written = 0;
        while (written < sizeInBytes())
            written += channel.write(buffer);
        buffer.reset();
        return written;
    }
After
    /**
     * Write all records to the given channel, including any partial records at the end.
     *
     * <p><strong>Behavior:</strong> Writes the entire buffer contents to the channel in potentially multiple
     * write operations, continuing until all bytes are written or an exception is raised. Uses the channel's
     * {@link GatheringByteChannel#write(ByteBuffer)} method, which may write fewer bytes than requested.
     *
     * <p><strong>Buffer State:</strong> The underlying buffer's position is preserved using mark/reset, so this
     * method has no side effects on the buffer state.
     *
     * <p><strong>Partial Records:</strong> Unlike {@link #validBytes()}, this method includes partial or potentially
     * corrupt record batches at the end of the buffer.
     *
     * @param channel the {@link GatheringByteChannel} to write to (e.g., socket, file channel)
     * @return the total number of bytes written
     * @throws IOException if an I/O error occurs during writing
     */
    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {
        buffer.mark();
        int written = 0;
        while (written < sizeInBytes())
            written += channel.write(buffer);
        buffer.reset();
        return written;
    }
19. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Validates the header of the first batch and returns batch size.
     * @return first batch size including LOG_OVERHEAD if buffer contains header up to
     *         magic byte, null otherwise
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if record size or magic is invalid
     */
    public Integer firstBatchSize() {
        if (buffer.remaining() < HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC)
            return null;
        return new ByteBufferLogInputStream(buffer, Integer.MAX_VALUE).nextBatchSize();
    }
After
    /**
     * Get the size of the first batch in the buffer, if it can be determined.
     *
     * <p><strong>Validation:</strong> This method parses and validates the first batch header up to and including
     * the magic byte. The returned size includes the {@link Records#LOG_OVERHEAD} (8-byte leading offset field) plus
     * the batch length from the header.
     *
     * <p><strong>Partial Batches:</strong> Returns {@code null} if the buffer contains fewer bytes than required to
     * read the header up to the magic byte (12 bytes total). This indicates a partial batch at the end.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> Reads only the batch header; does not validate or parse record contents.
     * Useful for framing batches when reading from streams with unknown boundaries.
     *
     * @return the total size of the first batch (including LOG_OVERHEAD) if the header is complete and valid,
     *         {@code null} if the buffer contains only a partial header
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if the batch size field indicates an invalid length or the magic byte is
     *                                unrecognized
     */
    public Integer firstBatchSize() {
        if (buffer.remaining() < HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC)
            return null;
        return new ByteBufferLogInputStream(buffer, Integer.MAX_VALUE).nextBatchSize();
    }
20. WRITE /workspace/documentation.md
# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Documentation Summary

**Repository:** apache/kafka
**Date:** March 1, 2026
**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`

## Overview

This document summarizes the comprehensive Javadoc documentation generated for Kafka's three critical record batch serialization classes:
- `RecordBatch` (interface)
- `DefaultRecordBatch` (magic v2+ implementation)
- `MemoryRecords` (in-memory batch collection)

These classes form the foundation of Kafka's message encoding and are essential for wire protocol compatibility, log storage, and broker-side record processing (compaction, filtering).

---

## 1. RecordBatch (Interface)

**File:** `RecordBatch.java` (lines 28-58)

### Class-Level Documentation Enhanced

Added comprehensive class-level Javadoc that includes:

- **Purpose:** Container for records using Kafka's binary encoding format
- **Thread Safety:** Explicitly stated as immutable and thread-safe for read operations, with notes about MutableRecordBatch for broker use
- **Role in Kafka Protocol:** Explains batch structure (header, records, CRC), batch-level compression, and producer metadata for exactly-once semantics
- **Performance Characteristics:** Documents lazy decompression and streaming iteration for memory efficiency
- **Cross-references:** Links to DefaultRecordBatch, MutableRecordBatch, and MemoryRecords

### Methods Documented

| Method | Enhancement |
|--------|-------------|
| `isValid()` | Added details on checksum verification cost and use cases |
| `ensureValid()` | Documented exception behavior and relationship to isValid() |
| `streamingIterator()` | Comprehensive performance guide for memory-efficient decompression; detailed BufferSupplier usage |
| All existing methods | Preserve
... (truncated)

      

      

        Bash Commands (4)
        
1. $ find /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record -type f -name "*.java" | head -20
2. $ ls -la /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/
3. $ find /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record -name "*RecordBatch*"
4. $ wc -l /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
